title: Smash Cheeseburger
subtitle: Thin beef patties pressed flat on a hot griddle, with American cheese and all the condiments
description: The smash burger is the American diner standard: a ball of beef mince placed on a screaming-hot griddle or cast-iron pan, then pressed flat with a spatula in the first 30 seconds of cooking. The contact between the thin patty and the flat, extremely hot surface produces the Maillard crust — the dark, craggy, flavour-rich exterior that distinguishes a great burger from a steamed one. Two thin patties with one slice of American cheese melted between them is the classic double. The technique takes under ten minutes once the pan is hot.
sourceNotes: The smash burger technique is associated with American diner cooking from the mid-twentieth century, particularly roadside lunch counters across the Midwest. The method of pressing a ball of beef flat onto a griddle within seconds of contact is the defining technique of the style, distinct from the thick-pattied California burger tradition.

recipeTools.notes: Or a flat griddle — the surface must be absolutely flat and scorching hot for the smash technique to work.
A wide, flat metal spatula — a fish slice or burger spatula. You need to press down hard: a wooden spoon or rounded spatula is not suitable.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical reaction between amino acids and sugars that occurs when food is heated to high temperatures, producing hundreds of flavour compounds and the characteristic dark-brown crust on seared meat.

ingredients.notes: 20% fat — do not go leaner or the patties will dry out. Divide into 4 balls of 100g each.
One slice per patty — melts best. Cheddar also works.
Added to the patty surface immediately before smashing — not mixed in beforehand
Or soft sesame-seed rolls — toasted cut-side down in a dry pan

steps: Divide the beef mince into 4 balls of 100g each. Do not season or compact them — keep them loose.
Toast the bun cut-sides down in a dry pan until golden. Set aside.
Heat a cast-iron pan or griddle over the highest heat for 3–4 minutes until smoking. Do not add oil.

Place two mince balls in the pan. Immediately season the top of each with salt and pepper. Smash them flat with the back of a wide spatula, pressing hard and holding for 10 seconds. The patties should be about 5 mm thick and have ragged, craggy edges.
Cook for 90 seconds without moving — the Maillard crust must form before you attempt to release the patty. The edges will turn grey and the surface will begin to look dry.

Flip with a metal spatula, scraping up all the crust. Place a slice of cheese on each patty immediately. Cook for 45–60 seconds. Stack one patty on top of the other while still in the pan so the cheese melts between them. Remove.
Repeat with the remaining two balls for the second burger.
Assemble: spread the condiments on the bun. Add lettuce, tomato, the double patty, onion, and pickles. Serve immediately.

content.text: The pan must be at its absolute hottest. The

that creates the crust only happens above 140 °C on the surface of the meat — and happens fastest, producing the most flavour, at 180 °C+. A cast-iron pan takes 3–4 minutes to reach this temperature. Heat it properly before any beef goes in.
Toast the buns, heat the pan

Divide {{beef-mince}} of mince into 4 loose balls of 100g each. Toast the bun cut-sides in a dry pan. Set aside. Heat a cast-iron pan or griddle over the highest heat for 3–4 minutes until smoking. Do not add oil.

Place two mince balls in the pan. Season the top with salt and pepper and immediately press flat with a wide spatula — press hard and hold for 10 seconds. The patties should be about 5 mm thick with ragged edges. Cook for 90 seconds without moving. Flip, scraping up all the crust. Add a slice of cheese to each patty. Cook for 45–60 seconds, then stack one patty on top of the other so the cheese melts between. Remove and repeat for the second burger.
Spread mustard, ketchup, and mayonnaise on the bun. Add a lettuce leaf, a slice of tomato, the double patty, sliced white onion, and pickles. Serve immediately.
